title: Kitsch Isn't Bad Taste — It's the Aesthetic Regime of a World Without Transcendence

source.original: The Post-Christian Condition – The Abrahamic Metacritique (Critique and Digest)

body_html:
Kitsch is usually dismissed as a problem of taste — the cheap, the sentimental, the mass-produced. But this misses what kitsch actually is: the necessary aesthetic form produced when human beings still structured for transcendence, still requiring ceremony that points beyond itself, are left with nothing but their own reaching. It is not an accident of industrial production but the cultural signature of the post-Christian condition.

The inversion at kitsch's core is precise. Beauty was once epistemically downstream of goodness — you perceived the beautiful because there was genuine contact with reality, traces of an actual encounter with the structure of things. Kitsch reverses this: the emotional effect becomes primary. The feeling of beauty, sublimity, or moral inspiration substitutes for the reality those feelings once indicated. Does it move you? Then it is working correctly. This is now the operating logic of moral, spiritual, and political discourse alike — the insistence on the primacy of feeling, the treatment of being moved as the criterion of being right.

Because the ground that would produce genuine transcendence is absent, kitsch must compensate through escalation: more sentiment, more spectacle, more intensity. The hip-hop star's cross with no relationship to the crucifixion, the Beatles shopping for transcendence in India with the same fundamental unseriousness as shopping for furniture, the entire wellness industry offering pastoral functions stripped of any claim about the actual structure of reality — all are expressions of the same structural impossibility. The infinite cannot be delivered through finite means.

The most literal incarnation may be the world of cosmetic surgery — lip fillers, stretched skin, reconstituted faces. This is not vanity in the old sense. It is kitsch in flesh: finite and rational means deployed against the infinite demand for vitality, the manufacturing of the effect of life where life is diminishing. It escapes nothing. It is, as the argument goes, the stench of death wearing the appearance of its opposite — the post-Christian condition's most intimate self-portrait.

What makes kitsch so difficult to see clearly is that it is not a deviation from the post-Christian condition but its defining form. The neurotic cycle of stimulation and renewed hunger is not a malfunction. It is exactly what happens when the romantic impulse — the genuine human reach toward the infinite — is converted into a process of consumption. The escalation is both kitsch's signature and its confession: it is not working. It was never going to work.
